message:
bench(core): add criterion benchmark for myopic policy decision path (process_triage-dki.2)

Benchmarks decide_action (single candidate + batch 1k), decide_from_belief,
and compute_loss_table with varied belief states and feasibility constraints.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"process_triage-aii.7.8.2","title":"Real-system CLI run (interactive) E2E","description":"## Objective
Test 'pt run' (interactive mode) logic without mocking internal state (using PTY if possible or dry-run).

## Scope
- pt-core run --dry-run --robot (to simulate selection without TUI)
- Verify plan generation against live processes.

## Implementation
- Spawn a known 'test-like' process (e.g. sleep 3600).
- Run 'pt-core run --robot --dry-run'.
- Verify the test process appears in the plan.
- Verify log events for planning.

## Acceptance
- Verifies integration of scan + plan + decision.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T18:06:22.093173296Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T18:29:07.126739634Z","closed_at":"2026-01-15T18:29:07.126741829Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-aii.7.8.2","depends_on_id":"process_triage-aii.7.8","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"process_triage-aii.8","title":"Implement fuzz testing for proc parsers and config loading","description":"## Overview
Add fuzz testing for security-critical parsing code, particularly /proc file parsers and config loaders.

## Background
Process triage parses untrusted input from /proc filesystem and user config files. Fuzz testing catches edge cases that could cause panics, hangs, or incorrect behavior.

## Scope

### 1. Target Modules
- \`collect/proc_parsers.rs\`: /proc/stat, /proc/statm, /proc/io, etc.
- \`collect/network.rs\`: /proc/net/tcp, /proc/net/unix parsing
- \`config/priors.rs\`: priors.json parsing
- \`config/policy.rs\`: policy.json parsing
- \`redact/detect.rs\`: Secret detection regex
- \`bundle/reader.rs\`: .ptb bundle parsing

### 2. Fuzz Harness Setup
- Use cargo-fuzz with libfuzzer (primary)
- AFL++ integration as alternative
- Each target module has dedicated harness
- Harnesses in \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/\`

### 3. Corpus Collection
- Sample real /proc outputs from various systems (Ubuntu, Fedora, Alpine, Arch)
- Edge case configs (empty, huge, malformed JSON)
- Known problematic inputs from bug reports
- Generated corner cases (max values, special characters)
- Store in \`fuzz/corpus/\`

### 4. Coverage Goals
- All pub fn that parse strings/bytes
- Input validation paths
- Error handling paths
- UTF-8 edge cases
- Integer overflow scenarios

### 5. CI Integration
- OSS-Fuzz integration for continuous fuzzing
- CI runs fuzz tests (time-limited: 60s per target)
- Crash reproduction in CI
- Corpus synced from OSS-Fuzz

---

## Testing Requirements

### Fuzz Targets
- **File**: \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/proc_stat.rs\`
  - Target: parse_proc_stat, parse_proc_statm
  - Input: arbitrary bytes simulating /proc content
  - Assertion: no panic, no hang (timeout 10s)

- **File**: \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/proc_io.rs\`
  - Target: parse_proc_io
  - Input: arbitrary /proc/PID/io content
  - Assertion: returns Ok or Err, never panic

- **File**: \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/config_priors.rs\`
  - Target: Priors::load
  - Input: arbitrary JSON bytes
  - Assertion: no panic, graceful error for invalid input

- **File**: \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/config_policy.rs\`
  - Target: Policy::load
  - Input: arbitrary JSON bytes
  - Assertion: graceful handling

- **File**: \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/network_parser.rs\`
  - Target: parse_tcp_connections, parse_unix_sockets
  - Input: /proc/net/* content
  - Assertion: no panic

- **File**: \`fuzz/fuzz_targets/bundle_reader.rs\`
  - Target: Bundle::read
  - Input: arbitrary bytes (potentially encrypted)
  - Assertion: graceful error for invalid bundles

### Crash Reproduction
- **File**: \`fuzz/reproduce/\`
- Each crash artifact saved with:
  - Input bytes that caused crash
  - Stack trace
  - Test case to reproduce
- Regression tests generated from crashes

### Coverage Tracking
- Use cargo-fuzz coverage mode
- Report coverage delta from fuzzing
- Track which functions hit by fuzz vs unit tests

---

## Logging Specifications

### Structured Log Events
| Event | Level | Fields | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| \`fuzz.session_start\` | INFO | target, duration, corpus_size | Fuzz session begins |
| \`fuzz.crash_found\` | ERROR | target, input_hash, stack_trace | Crash detected |
| \`fuzz.corpus_grow\` | DEBUG | target, new_inputs | Corpus expanded |
| \`fuzz.coverage\` | INFO | target, lines_covered, branches_covered | Coverage report |
| \`fuzz.session_end\` | INFO | target, iterations, crashes_found | Session complete |

---

## Error Handling

### For Fuzzing Infrastructure
| Scenario | Detection | Recovery | Action |
|----------|-----------|----------|--------|
| Fuzz crash | Non-zero exit | Save artifact | Create regression test |
| Timeout | Process killed | Save input | Investigate for DoS |
| OOM | Memory limit | Save input | Investigate memory leak |
| Coverage plateau | No new paths | Reduce duration | Move on |

### For Target Code (what fuzzing should catch)
- Panic on malformed input → FIX to return error
- Hang on crafted input → FIX to add timeout/limit
- Memory exhaustion → FIX to add size limits
- Integer overflow → FIX to use checked arithmetic

---

## Performance Targets
- CI fuzz run: 60s per target (discovery mode)
- OSS-Fuzz: 24h continuous (deep mode)
- Corpus size: < 10MB per target
- No timeouts at 10s per input

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Fuzz harnesses for all proc_parsers
- [ ] Fuzz harnesses for config loading
- [ ] Fuzz harnesses for bundle reading
- [ ] CI runs fuzz tests (time-limited)
- [ ] OSS-Fuzz integration setup (or similar)
- [ ] No panics on any fuzz input (after fixes)
- [ ] Corpus committed to repo
- [ ] Regression tests for any crashes found
- [ ] Coverage report shows parsing paths hit

## Dependencies
- cargo-fuzz installation
- libfuzzer or AFL++
- Part of: Testing Infrastructure (process_triage-aii)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CobaltBeaver","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T18:53:42.176941680Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-08T08:08:09.578875722Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T08:08:09.578725142Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-aii.8","depends_on_id":"process_triage-aii","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"process_triage-aii.9","title":"Implement chaos testing for daemon mode resilience","description":"## Overview
Implement chaos testing for daemon mode resilience under adverse conditions.

## Background
The dormant daemon (ptd) runs continuously and must handle failures gracefully: disk full, network partitions, OOM pressure, signal storms, etc.

## Scope

### 1. Fault Injection Framework
- Framework for injecting faults during E2E tests
- Faults: disk full, slow disk, network timeout, SIGTERM storm
- Controllable via environment variables or test API
- Clean recovery after test

### 2. Resource Exhaustion Tests
- **Disk full**: Fill temp partition, verify daemon handles write failures
- **Memory pressure**: Limit cgroup memory, verify graceful degradation
- **File descriptor exhaustion**: Limit FDs, verify daemon doesn't hang
- **CPU starvation**: Nice to 19 + CPU limit, verify continued operation

### 3. Signal Handling Tests
- SIGTERM: Clean shutdown within timeout
- SIGINT: Same as SIGTERM
- SIGHUP: Reload configuration
- SIGUSR1/2: Status dump / force scan
- Signal storm: 1000 signals in 1s, no crash

### 4. Recovery Tests
- Daemon restart after OOM kill: recovers state
- Daemon restart after SIGKILL: clean restart
- Config file deleted: graceful error
- Telemetry lake corrupted: rebuild or bypass

### 5. Network Partition Tests
- Fleet mode: Coordinator unreachable
- OTLP endpoint: Exporter unavailable
- GitHub API: Update check fails
- All network gone: Offline operation

### 6. Time-Related Tests
- Clock skew: System time jumps forward/backward
- NTP correction: Large time adjustment
- Leap second: (if applicable)
- Long uptime: 30+ day simulation

---

## Testing Requirements

### Chaos Test Suite
- **File**: \`test/chaos/daemon_chaos.bats\`
- Infrastructure: Docker with cgroups control
- Each test: setup → inject fault → verify behavior → cleanup

### Resource Exhaustion Tests
- **File**: \`test/chaos/resource_exhaustion.bats\`
- Test cases:
  - \`test_disk_full\`: Fill /tmp, verify daemon logs error, continues scan
  - \`test_memory_pressure\`: 50MB limit, verify no OOM on normal load
  - \`test_fd_exhaustion\`: 100 FD limit, verify graceful queuing
  - \`test_cpu_starvation\`: 5% CPU limit, verify scan completes (slowly)

### Signal Handling Tests
- **File**: \`test/chaos/signal_handling.bats\`
- Test cases:
  - \`test_sigterm_clean_shutdown\`: Exit within 5s, no data loss
  - \`test_sighup_reload\`: Config reloaded without restart
  - \`test_signal_storm\`: 1000 SIGUSR1 in 1s, no crash
  - \`test_sigkill_recovery\`: Restart recovers state

### Recovery Tests
- **File**: \`test/chaos/recovery.bats\`
- Test cases:
  - \`test_restart_after_oom\`: State recovered from persistence
  - \`test_corrupt_state\`: Daemon rebuilds from scratch
  - \`test_missing_config\`: Clear error, defaults used

### Network Partition Tests
- **File**: \`test/chaos/network_partition.bats\`
- Test cases (using network namespaces or iptables):
  - \`test_otlp_unreachable\`: Traces buffered locally
  - \`test_fleet_coordinator_down\`: Node operates standalone
  - \`test_github_unreachable\`: Update check skipped

### Long-Running Tests
- **File**: \`test/chaos/longevity.bats\`
- Test cases:
  - \`test_24h_operation\`: No memory leak, no FD leak
  - \`test_1000_scans\`: Performance doesn't degrade
  - \`test_clock_jump\`: Time-based logic handles discontinuity

---

## Logging Specifications

### Structured Log Events for Chaos
| Event | Level | Fields | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| \`chaos.fault_inject\` | WARN | fault_type, parameters | Fault injected |
| \`chaos.fault_clear\` | INFO | fault_type | Fault cleared |
| \`chaos.recovery_start\` | INFO | from_state | Recovery begins |
| \`chaos.recovery_complete\` | INFO | duration_ms, data_loss | Recovery done |

### Daemon Resilience Logs
| Event | Level | Fields | Purpose |
|-------|-------|--------|---------|
| \`daemon.resource_warning\` | WARN | resource, current, limit | Approaching limit |
| \`daemon.resource_exhausted\` | ERROR | resource, action_taken | Limit hit |
| \`daemon.signal_received\` | INFO | signal, action | Signal handled |
| \`daemon.graceful_shutdown\` | INFO | reason, duration_ms | Clean exit |

---

## Error Handling

### Expected Daemon Behavior Under Chaos
| Fault | Expected Behavior |
|-------|------------------|
| Disk full | Log error, skip persistence, continue scan |
| OOM kill | Restart recovers state, no duplicate actions |
| Network down | Buffer telemetry, operate offline |
| Signal storm | Process signals, no crash, may drop excess |
| Clock jump | Adjust timers, log warning |
| Config deleted | Use defaults, log warning |

### Test Verification
- Daemon exits cleanly (exit code 0) on controlled faults
- No data corruption after any fault
- State recoverable after crash

---

## Performance Targets
- Graceful shutdown: < 5s
- Recovery after crash: < 10s to operational
- Resource overhead under pressure: < 2x normal

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Fault injection framework operational
- [ ] Resource exhaustion tests pass
- [ ] Signal handling tests pass
- [ ] Recovery tests pass
- [ ] Network partition tests pass
- [ ] 24h longevity test passes (no leaks)
- [ ] All chaos tests documented
- [ ] CI runs subset of chaos tests

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Dormant daemon (process_triage-b4v)
- Part of: Testing Infrastructure (process_triage-aii)
- Requires: Docker with cgroups v2 for resource limits","notes":"Added first chaos-style daemon resilience coverage in crates/pt-core/tests/e2e_daemon_dormant.rs: daemon_signal_storm_remains_alive_during_storm (rapid SIGUSR1 burst with deterministic teardown). Verified targeted and full e2e_daemon_dormant suite pass with --features daemon. Remaining scope still includes resource/network/long-run chaos suites.","status":"in_progress","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"DustyStone","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T18:53:43.169291237Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-13T17:39:23.736421445Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-aii.9","depends_on_id":"process_triage-aii","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"process_triage-aip","title":"Release Automation with Checksums","description":"## Overview
Automate releases so users can install/verify the project safely and easily.

This epic must support the plan’s two-layer architecture:
- `pt` (bash wrapper)
- `pt-core` (Rust monolithic binary)

## Release Trigger
- Git tag push: `vX.Y.Z`

## What a Release Must Produce
### Source-level artifacts
- `pt` wrapper script
- `install.sh`

### `pt-core` binaries (minimum initial matrix)
- Linux x86_64
- Linux aarch64
- macOS x86_64
- macOS aarch64

(Windows can be added later if/when supported.)

### Integrity artifacts
- `checksums.sha256` containing SHA-256 for every uploaded file
- Optional per-file `.sha256` files for installer-friendly verification

## Version Consistency
Releases must be internally consistent:
- Tag version matches:
  - the shared `VERSION` source of truth (see `process_triage-nk1`)
  - `pt-core --version`
  - wrapper-reported version (if wrapper exposes one)

CI should block mismatches so broken releases are impossible.

## Security / Supply Chain Considerations
- Checksums are generated in CI from the exact uploaded bytes.
- Release notes include verification instructions.
- Optional future upgrade: artifact signing (cosign/minisign) and SBOM generation.

## Success Criteria
- A tagged release is fully installable on supported platforms.
- Verification is straightforward (checksums) and documented.
- Releases are reproducible and don’t ship mismatched versions.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Tag push creates a GitHub release with wrapper + installer + all `pt-core` binaries.
- [ ] Release fails fast on version mismatch.
- [ ] Checksums are generated and uploaded, and match downloaded artifacts.
- [ ] Install flow can verify `pt-core` via checksums (supports `VERIFY=1`).

## Notes
- Keep artifact naming conventions stable; installer logic should not chase moving targets.

Version consistency enforced via CI checks.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-aip","depends_on_id":"process_triage-h89","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-arb5","title":"Implement posterior predictive checks (PPC) for model validation","description":"## Section 4.14 - Posterior Predictive Checks

**Purpose**: Validate Bayesian models by comparing observed data to simulated data from the posterior. If model is correct, observed data should 'look like' simulated data.

**Mathematical Background**:
- PPC: y_rep ~ P(y|D) = ∫ P(y|θ) P(θ|D) dθ
- Test statistic: T(y) could be mean, variance, max, or domain-specific
- p-value: P(T(y_rep) ≥ T(y_obs)) - extreme values suggest model misfit
- Calibration: P(y ∈ CI_α) should equal α across many predictions
- WAIC: Watanabe-Akaike IC for model comparison: -2(lppd - p_waic)

**Implementation Requirements**:
1. `generate_ppc_samples(model, posterior_samples, n_rep)` - Draw y_rep
2. `ppc_test(y_obs, y_rep, statistic)` - Compute posterior p-value
3. `calibration_check(predictions, outcomes)` - Reliability diagram
4. `compute_waic(log_likelihoods)` - Model comparison metric

**Why This Matters for pt**:
If our Beta-Binomial model predicts 70% kill but we see 30% user agreement, the model is miscalibrated. PPC detects this before deployment.

**Integration Points**:
- Model validation pipeline (Section 4)
- Prior sensitivity analysis (Section 2.3)
- Confidence reporting (Section 7.1)

**Test Requirements**:
- Verify PPC detects known model misspecification
- Verify calibration check catches over/under-confident models
- Verify WAIC selects correct model on synthetic data","notes":"Duplicate/out-of-tree bead.  {"id":"process_triage-asdq","title":"Implement failure recovery trees for agent error handling","description":"## Overview
Implement structured failure recovery trees that provide deterministic fallback paths for every action type when actions fail.

## From Plan Section 6.2

### Failure Categories
- `permission_denied`: Insufficient privileges
- `process_not_found`: PID doesn't exist (already dead or wrong target)
- `process_protected`: Kernel/system protection blocked action
- `timeout`: Action didn't complete in expected time
- `supervisor_conflict`: Supervisor restarted the process
- `resource_conflict`: Resource held by another process
- `unexpected_error`: Unknown/unanticipated failure

### Recovery Tree Structure
Each recommended action includes a failure recovery tree:
```json
{
  'action': 'kill',
  'target': {'pid': 1234, 'start_id': '1705312200.1234'},
  'recovery_tree': {
    'permission_denied': {
      'diagnosis': 'Current user lacks permission to signal this process',
      'alternatives': [
        {'action': 'sudo_kill', 'requirements': ['sudo_available'], 'command': 'sudo kill -TERM 1234'},
        {'action': 'escalate_to_user', 'message': 'Process owned by another user; requires elevated privileges'}
      ]
    },
    'process_not_found': {
      'diagnosis': 'Process no longer exists',
      'verification': 'Check if goal achieved (process may have exited naturally)',
      'alternatives': [
        {'action': 'verify_goal', 'command': 'pt agent verify --session <id>'},
        {'action': 'check_respawn', 'note': 'If supervised, check if replacement spawned'}
      ]
    },
    'timeout': {
      'diagnosis': 'Process did not terminate within grace period',
      'alternatives': [
        {'action': 'sigkill', 'command': 'kill -9 1234', 'note': 'Escalate to SIGKILL'},
        {'action': 'investigate_d_state', 'condition': 'if state == D', 'note': 'Process may be in uninterruptible sleep'}
      ]
    },
    'supervisor_conflict': {
      'diagnosis': 'Process was killed but immediately respawned by supervisor',
      'alternatives': [
        {'action': 'stop_supervisor', 'command': 'systemctl stop myapp.service'},
        {'action': 'mask_and_kill', 'command': 'systemctl mask myapp.service && systemctl stop myapp.service'}
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

### Agent Workflow with Failure Recovery
1. Attempt primary action
2. If success: verify outcome, report success
3. If failure:
   a. Classify failure type
   b. Look up recovery tree for (action, failure_type)
   c. Check if any alternative has met requirements
   d. If yes: attempt first viable alternative
   e. If no alternatives viable: report failure with diagnosis
4. Limit recovery attempts (default: 3) to prevent infinite loops
5. Log all attempts and outcomes for session audit

### Recovery Hints in Output
```json
{
  'action_results': [
    {
      'target': {'pid': 1234},
      'action': 'kill',
      'result': 'failed',
      'failure_type': 'supervisor_conflict',
      'attempts': [
        {'action': 'kill', 'result': 'process_respawned', 'time_ms': 150},
        {'action': 'kill', 'result': 'process_respawned', 'time_ms': 145}
      ],
      'diagnosis': 'Process is supervised by systemd (myapp.service) with Restart=always',
      'recovery_hint': {
        'recommended_action': 'systemctl stop myapp.service',
        'explanation': 'Stop the supervisor to prevent respawn',
        'reversibility': 'Service can be restarted with: systemctl start myapp.service'
      },
      'agent_next_step': 'Run suggested supervisor command or report to user'
    }
  ]
}
```

### Common Recovery Patterns
| Failure | Primary Recovery | Fallback |
|---------|------------------|----------|
| Permission denied | sudo kill | Escalate to user |
| Respawns after kill | Stop supervisor | Mask unit + stop |
| SIGTERM ignored | SIGKILL | Investigate D-state |
| D-state process | Wait + report | Check IO device |
| Cgroup op fails | Check cgroup v2 | Fall back to renice |
| Docker won't stop | docker kill | docker rm -f |

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Failure classification works for all types
- [ ] Recovery tree structure defined per action type
- [ ] Alternative actions have requirements checks
- [ ] Retry limit enforced
- [ ] All attempts logged
- [ ] Recovery hints included in output

## Dependencies
- Phase 6 (action execution)
- Phase 10 (supervisor detection)

## Technical Notes
- Recovery trees are static but can reference dynamic conditions
- Requirements checking must be fast
- Logging must capture full attempt history","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"BlueBird","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T08:57:11.870472259Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T20:53:06.322274470Z","closed_at":"2026-01-15T20:53:06.322274470Z","close_reason":"Implemented recovery tree executor with RequirementChecker, RecoverySession, and RecoveryExecutor. 24 unit tests passing.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-asdq","depends_on_id":"process_triage-sj6","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"process_triage-dj9","title":"Implement responsive TUI layout system","description":"## Overview
Build a responsive TUI layout system that adapts to terminal dimensions.

## Background
The plan specifies a premium TUI experience with multiple panels: process list, evidence detail, action preview, and status bar. This must work in terminals from 80x24 to ultra-wide displays.

## Why It Matters
Users run pt in varied environments: tiny SSH sessions, tmux panes, full-screen terminals. A responsive layout ensures information is accessible regardless of terminal size, without horizontal scrolling or truncation that loses critical data.

## Technical Approach
1. Implement layout engine with flexible panel sizing
2. Define breakpoints (minimal, compact, standard, wide)
3. Support panel collapse/expand based on space
4. Maintain readable minimum widths
5. Use Ratatui's constraint-based layout system

## Layout Modes
- **Minimal (80x24)**: Single panel, tab navigation
- **Compact (120x30)**: Process list + detail split
- **Standard (160x40)**: Three-panel layout
- **Wide (200+)**: Full layout with side panels

## Panel Priority (when space constrained)
1. Process list (always visible, minimum 40 cols)
2. Status bar (always visible, 1 row)
3. Evidence summary (collapse to icons if needed)
4. Action preview (hide in minimal mode)
5. Galaxy-brain panel (wide mode only)

## Responsive Behaviors
- Text truncation with ellipsis
- Column hiding based on width
- Row consolidation for height
- Scroll indicators when content exceeds view

## Success Criteria
- Layout adapts smoothly to resize
- No horizontal scrolling required
- All modes functional and usable
- Transitions between modes clean

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-dki","title":"EPIC: Performance and Scalability","description":"## Overview
Performance and scalability work to ensure `pt-core` can scan and reason about **very large process tables** without becoming a resource hog itself.

This epic supports:
- fleet mode (many hosts; large inventories)
- dormant daemon mode (continuous monitoring)
- premium UX (fast, responsive TUI)

## Plan References
- Plan §3.1 / §3.7 / §3.8 (collection, dormant, fleet)
- Plan §8.1 (“tool becomes the hog” pitfall)

## Targets (Guidance; tune via benchmarks)
- Quick scan: ~10k processes in ~1s (bounded allocations)
- Deep scan: budgeted and probe-selected; do not “deep scan everything”
- Daemon: low steady-state overhead with cooldowns

## Key Strategies
- Incremental scanning and caching keyed by stable identity tuples.
- Parallel /proc reads with strict backpressure.
- Lazy/probe-selected deep evidence collection driven by VOI/budget policy.
- Compact data structures; dedupe/harden string storage under redaction constraints.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Benchmarks exist and gate regressions.
- [ ] Scanning is bounded in time/memory and respects overhead budgets.
- [ ] Daemon mode runs with low sustained CPU and avoids self-induced load spikes.

 {"id":"process_triage-dna","title":"Add version consistency check to CI","description":"## Purpose
Add a CI job that verifies the VERSION file matches the VERSION constant in the pt script.

## Parent Epic
GitHub Actions CI/CD Pipeline (process_triage-68c)

## Depends On
- Create VERSION file as single source of truth

## Why This Check?
Common release bug: update VERSION file but forget to update script (or vice versa).
- CI catches this before merge
- Release workflow also checks this before publishing

## Implementation

### Add to ci.yml
```yaml
  version-check:
    name: Version Consistency
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Check VERSION file exists
        run: |
          if [[ \! -f VERSION ]]; then
            echo "::error::VERSION file not found"
            exit 1
          fi
      
      - name: Verify version consistency
        run: |
          file_version=$(cat VERSION | tr -d '\n')
          script_version=$(grep '^VERSION=' pt | head -1 | cut -d'"' -f2)
          
          echo "VERSION file: $file_version"
          echo "pt script:    $script_version"
          
          if [[ "$file_version" \!= "$script_version" ]]; then
            echo ""
            echo "::error::Version mismatch\!"
            echo "VERSION file contains: $file_version"
            echo "pt script contains:    $script_version"
            echo ""
            echo "Please update both to match."
            exit 1
          fi
          
          echo "✓ Versions match: $file_version"
      
      - name: Validate version format
        run: |
          version=$(cat VERSION | tr -d '\n')
          
          # Check semver format (X.Y.Z)
          if [[ \! "$version" =~ ^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$ ]]; then
            echo "::error::Invalid version format: $version"
            echo "Expected: X.Y.Z (e.g., 1.2.3)"
            exit 1
          fi
          
          echo "✓ Version format valid: $version"
```

## Error Output
GitHub Actions `::error::` syntax creates annotations visible in PR UI:
- Red error badge
- Clickable link to the check
- Clear message about what's wrong

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Job checks VERSION file exists
- [ ] Job compares VERSION file to script constant
- [ ] Job validates semver format
- [ ] Clear error messages on mismatch
- [ ] PR blocked if versions don't match

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.

 {"id":"process_triage-dnrq","title":"Implement session comparison and diff","description":"## Task: Session Comparison and Diff

### Description
Implement comparison between session snapshots to track changes over time.

### Requirements
1. **Diff Output**
   ```
   Session Diff: session_001 → session_002
   Time: 2024-01-15 10:00 → 2024-01-15 14:30
   Duration: 4h 30m
   
   SUMMARY:
     Candidates: 15 → 23 (+8)
     Kills: 3 → 8 (+5)
     Spares: 2 → 5 (+3)
     Memory freed: 4.2 GB → 12.1 GB (+7.9 GB)
   
   NEW PROCESSES (8):
     + PID 12345: bun test --watch (score: 72)
     + PID 12346: next dev (score: 45)
     ...
   
   CHANGED PROCESSES (5):
     Δ PID 11111: score 35 → 72 (+37), now KILL
     Δ PID 11112: score 65 → 25 (-40), now SPARE
     ...
   
   REMOVED PROCESSES (3):
     - PID 10000: pytest (was score 80, killed)
     - PID 10001: node (was score 30, exited naturally)
     ...
   
   SCORE DISTRIBUTION CHANGE:
     KILL (≥60):   5 → 8 (+3)
     REVIEW (30-59): 6 → 10 (+4)
     SPARE (<30):  4 → 5 (+1)
   ```

2. **Diff Metrics**
   - Process count changes
   - Score changes per process
   - Recommendation changes
   - Resource changes (memory, CPU)
   - Outcome tracking (what happened to old candidates)

3. **JSON Diff Output**
   ```json
   {
     "from_session": "session_001",
     "to_session": "session_002",
     "duration_seconds": 16200,
     "changes": {
       "added": [{"pid": 12345, "score": 72, ...}],
       "removed": [{"pid": 10000, "score": 80, "outcome": "killed"}],
       "changed": [{"pid": 11111, "old_score": 35, "new_score": 72}]
     },
     "summary": {
       "candidate_delta": 8,
       "kill_delta": 5,
       "memory_freed_gb": 7.9
     }
   }
   ```

4. **CLI Interface**
   ```bash
   # Compare two session bundles
   pt session diff session1.tar.gz session2.tar.gz
   
   # Compare with current state
   pt session diff session1.tar.gz --current
   
   # JSON output for programmatic use
   pt session diff session1.tar.gz session2.tar.gz --format=json
   ```

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Diff correctly identifies added/removed/changed processes
- [ ] Score changes are highlighted
- [ ] Outcomes are tracked (killed/spared/exited)
- [ ] JSON diff is parseable by agents","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-15T09:07:56.984404826Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-15T10:54:38.825165458Z","closed_at":"2026-01-15T10:54:38.825165458Z","close_reason":"duplicate/outdated (canonical: process_triage-9k8)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"process_triage-dnrq","depends_on_id":"process_triage-bra","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:03Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"process_triage-dvi","title":"EPIC: Phase 8 - Safety, Policy, and Guardrails","description":"## Overview
Phase 8 implements safety guardrails, policy enforcement, and audit logging to ensure pt operates within defined boundaries.

## Background
The plan specifies that policy.json defines hard limits that cannot be overridden: protected process patterns, maximum concurrent kills, require_human_for_supervised, and loss matrix bounds. This phase implements the enforcement layer.

## Why It Matters
Safety guardrails prevent catastrophic mistakes. Even with perfect inference, bugs or misconfigurations could cause pt to recommend killing critical processes. Policy enforcement provides defense in depth.

## Phase Scope
1. Policy.json enforcement engine
2. Protected process pattern matching
3. Rate limiting (max concurrent kills, cooldown periods)
4. Audit logging with tamper detection
5. Dry-run mode implementation
6. Integration with system security (SELinux/AppArmor awareness)

## Key Safety Mechanisms
- **Protected patterns**: Regex list of never-kill processes (systemd, sshd, docker, etc.)
- **Rate limits**: Max 10 kills per minute, 100 per hour (configurable)
- **Supervisor protection**: Block auto-kill on supervised processes
- **Loss bounds**: Reject action if expected loss exceeds threshold
- **Audit trail**: Append-only log with cryptographic checksums

## Dependencies
- Phase 1: policy.json schema definition
- Phase 5: Decision theory (loss calculations)
- Phase 6: Action execution (enforcement point)

## Success Criteria
- Protected processes cannot be killed regardless of score
- Rate limits enforced and reported
- Audit log captures all decisions and actions
- Dry-run mode produces accurate predictions
- Policy violations logged with context

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Implements the full scope described above (no silent omissions).
- [ ] Handles edge/error cases described above with deterministic behavior.
- [ ] Includes unit tests for the core logic and the highest-risk edge cases.
- [ ] Emits structured logs/telemetry for key decisions and failures (as applicable).

## Test Plan
- Unit: golden tests for core logic; edge cases and error paths.
- Integration: exercise real tool/system interactions where applicable (or mocked equivalents).
- E2E: cover at least one representative user/agent workflow when applicable.
- Logging: tests validate presence/shape of critical log/telemetry fields.
